title: Getwork | PoliceStationRepUK — Find Accredited Police Station Reps

headings.text: Complete Guide: How to Get Work as a Police Station Representative
Need a Police Station Rep in Kent?

content: FREE WHILST TESTINGLast Updated: 2nd February 2026Complete Guide: How to Get Work as a Police Station RepresentativeA comprehensive 6-phase roadmap to building a sustainable freelance practice earning £40k-£80k+ annually.From setting up your first profile to scaling into a thriving business—everything you need to succeed as a freelance police station representative.Important Regulatory Notice: Probationary RepresentativesPlease note that this guide is for fully accredited representatives and solicitors only.Probationary representatives cannot work as freelance agents.Regulatory Citation: Under the LAA Police Station Register Arrangements and Standard Crime Contract, a Probationary Representative must be registered under a specific Supervising Solicitor at a specific provider. The supervising solicitor is personally responsible for the probationary rep's work. Therefore, a probationary rep cannot accept freelance instructions from other firms.Your 6-Phase Client Acquisition RoadmapPhase 1: Immediate Setup (Week 1)Essential groundwork to make yourself hireablePhase 2: Active Outreach (Weeks 1-4)Direct marketing to build your client basePhase 3: Responsive Service (Ongoing)Convert enquiries into bookings through speed and professionalismPhase 4: Pricing & Commercial SuccessUnderstanding legal aid rates and maximizing your incomePhase 5: Client Retention & GrowthTurn one-off jobs into repeat clients and referralsPhase 6: Scaling & SpecializationFrom solo rep to thriving business or recognized expert* Follow this structured approach to build a sustainable freelance practice generating £40k-£80k+ annuallyDetailed Work Acquisition StrategyPhase 1: Immediate Setup (Week 1)Essential groundwork to make yourself hireableOptimize Your Online PresenceYour digital profile is often the first—and sometimes only—impression firms getComplete your PoliceStationRepUK profile with professional photo, full contact details, ALL stations you can cover (not just locals), clear availability windows, accreditation status (CLSA/LCCSA membership number)Write a compelling 2-3 sentence bio highlighting your experience: years qualified, specialisms, notable case types handled, unique selling points (e.g., 'Experienced in youth justice with 200+ cases')List specific stations with realistic travel times: 'Birmingham Central (15 mins), Solihull (30 mins), Sutton Coldfield (25 mins)'—firms filter by station coverageSet up Google Business Profile: search 'your name + police station representative + [your area]' to claim/create listing; add phone, website, hoursCreate LinkedIn profile if you don't have one: professional headshot, detailed work history, skills endorsements from colleagues; join 'Criminal Defence Solicitors UK' and similar groupsCheck your digital footprint: Google yourself—delete or make private any unprofessional social media; firms WILL Google you before bookingSet Your Service Offering & RatesUnderstand the legal aid payment structure and what you can negotiateUnderstand LAA fixed fees: Firms receive £320 per police station attendance from the LAA (harmonised rate from Dec 2025 - previously varied by station)Know your market value: Freelance reps typically receive £100-£180 per attendance from firms, depending on experience, location, and time of dayNegotiate your fee with each firm: Your starting point depends on your experience (new rep £100-£120, established rep £120-£150, specialist/out-of-hours £150-£180+)Define your coverage: Which stations you'll attend, maximum travel distance, response times (e.g., 'within 90 minutes of call')Out-of-hours availability: Decide if you'll work evenings/weekends/nights; negotiate premium rates (e.g., +20-30% for 10pm-7am or bank holidays)Additional services: Some firms need file reviews, detailed attendance notes, or follow-up calls - clarify if these are included in your fee or billed separatelyCreate simple rate card (1-page PDF): Your attendance fees (standard/out-of-hours), stations covered, availability, travel policy, payment terms; send with every introductionPayment terms: Decide Net 14 days, Net 30 days, or payment on completion; be consistent and put it in writingRegister on All Relevant PlatformsCast a wide net—more visibility = more opportunitiesPoliceStationRepUK directory: ensure profile is complete and searchablePolice Station Rep UK Directory (policestationrepukdirectory.com): separate enhanced directory, paid listings availableCLSA/LCCSA member directories: update your profile to show you're actively seeking workLaw Society 'Find a Solicitor': if you're a solicitor, ensure criminal law is listed as practice areaLocal law society websites: many county law societies have rep directories (e.g., Kent Law Society, Sussex Law Society)Consider niche directories: Youth Justice specialists, Fraud Lawyers Association if you have relevant experienceSet Google Alerts for '[your area] police station representative' to monitor competitor activity and opportunitiesPhase 2: Active Outreach (Weeks 1-4)Direct marketing to build your client basePhase 3: Responsive Service (Ongoing)Convert enquiries into bookings through speed and professionalismPhase 4: Pricing & Commercial SuccessUnderstanding legal aid rates and maximizing your incomePhase 5: Client Retention & GrowthTurn one-off jobs into repeat clients and referralsPhase 6: Scaling & SpecializationFrom solo rep to thriving business or recognized expert Quick‑Start Checklist (this week)Complete your directory profile: photo, coverage, availability, contact details, accreditationJoin the WhatsApp group and enable notifications (mentions + direct replies)Create professional marketing materials: one-page services catalogue with your details, stations, ratesList 10 nearby firms and introduce yourself (email + call follow‑up)Understand LAA fixed fees (firms receive £320, you negotiate £100-£180)Create attendance note template to ensure consistent, professional documentationConfirm your availability windows (e.g., evenings/weekends) and response SLAsPrepare a "first contact" script for calls/messages Outreach Templates (copy/paste and personalise)WhatsApp / SMS (urgent cover)Hello [Name], I'm an accredited police station rep covering [Areas/Stations]. Available [times]. Experience with [offence types]. Can assist with [case brief]. Best contact: [phone/email]. Thanks, [Your Name].Intro Email to FirmsSubject: Police Station Representation – Availability in [County/Stations]Dear [Practice Manager/Name],I'm an accredited representative with experience covering [stations/force area]. I'm available [availability windows] and can assist with [types of matters]. Kind regards,[Your Name] | [Phone] | [Email] | [Link to profile] Follow up with a call within 48h Diary a polite check‑in after 1–2 weeks Coverage & AvailabilityList specific stations you can reach within 60–90 minutes; mention night‑time travel limitations if any.State realistic response times (e.g., "within 30 mins to custody call‑backs; 90 mins to station").Keep your profile's "stations served" and "availability" current; firms filter by these fields first.Coordinate with nearby reps for overflow/holiday cover to avoid turning firms away. Pricing & Billing BasicsUnderstand that firms receive approximately £260 per attendance from LAA - you negotiate your share with the firm (typically £80-£150).You cannot bill LAA directly - you work for firms who hold the LAA contracts.Confirm scope up‑front: travel, waiting, interview time, any additional services expected.Send a clear invoice within 24–48 hours with firm reference, attendance times, agreed fee.Keep simple records: date, station, client initials, offence, time on site, outcome, firm contact.Set polite payment terms (e.g., 14 days) and schedule a reminder cadence. Reputation & RetentionConfirm attendance promptly and be early—custody delays are common, reliability stands out.Send detailed, professional attendance notes to instructing solicitors within 2 hours—quality documentation is what firms pay for.Use templates for consistency but customize for each case—your notes may be reviewed in court months later.Remember: you're only as good as your last case—one poor attendance can undo months of good work.Protect confidentiality; never share case details unnecessarily.Ask satisfied firms if they're happy to contact you again; consistency builds repeat work. Safety & ComplianceOnly work within your accreditation and competence; if unsure, seek supervision or decline.Carry accreditation evidence; firms may ask for LCCSA/CLSA membership cards.Keep personal data secure; redact client identifiers in comms where not essential.Know local station procedures and PACE considerations; plan for overnight call‑outs.Helpful ResourcesBrowse the Reps DirectorySee how successful reps have set up their profiles.Join the WhatsApp GroupThe fastest way to get notified about available work.Access the Forms LibraryDownload essential forms like CRM1 and CRM2.Read Expert ArticlesPractical tips and legal insights for police station workCustody Note — The custody note app for freelance repsStructured notes with LAA code support, firm billing, PACE reviews, and PDF export. Works offline.🎁 PSR UK exclusive: 25% off with code A2MJY2NQTry Free Advertisement · custodynote.comReady to boost your career?Register for free and put these strategies into action.Create Your Free Profile Article Ref: GW-20251125-F6G7H8I9Written by Robert Cashman, Court and Police Station Duty Solicitor and Higher Court Advocate.Revised by Robert Cashman on 28th November 2025© 2026 PoliceStationRepUK.com. All rights reserved.
